title: Day 05 Interview Prep
description: Practice explaining SPA routing, nested routes, Link vs anchor, and dashboard layout architecture.

questions.question: What is client-side routing in React?
keyPoints: URL changes without full document reload
React Router maps URL to components
questions.sampleAnswer: Client-side routing updates the URL and swaps React components without reloading the page. React Router listens to URL changes and renders the matching route. Users get fast navigation and bookmarkable URLs like /settings.

questions.question: What is the difference between Link and a regular anchor tag?
questions.sampleAnswer: Link navigates within the SPA using client-side history. Regular <a href> triggers full page load — fine for external sites. I use Link for internal routes and <a target='_blank'> for external docs.

questions.question: What are nested routes?
questions.sampleAnswer: Nested routes let a parent layout — sidebar and navbar — wrap multiple child pages. Outlet marks where the child route renders. /dashboard/settings shows layout plus Settings content without duplicating chrome.

questions.question: Explain SPA vs traditional multi-page application.
keyPoints: MPA: server returns new HTML per request
questions.sampleAnswer: SPAs load once then route client-side — fast transitions, app-like feel. MPAs fetch new HTML per navigation — simpler SEO and smaller initial JS sometimes. React dashboards are typically SPAs; marketing sites might be MPA or hybrid.

questions.question: How does Outlet work with React Router v6?
questions.sampleAnswer: Outlet is a placeholder in the parent layout. React Router renders whichever child route matches the current URL into that slot. My DashboardLayout has Sidebar, Navbar, and main with Outlet — children swap inside main only.

questions.question: What is a closure and why does it matter in route components?
keyPoints: Function captures variables from outer scope
Event handlers close over render values
Stale closure with effects — Day 04 tie-in
questions.sampleAnswer: A closure is when a function remembers variables from where it was defined. Click handlers in ProfilePage close over props and state from that render. Understanding closures explains stale values in effects and callbacks — core JS for React.

questions.question: How would you structure routes for a growing admin app with 20+ pages?
questions.sampleAnswer: I group routes by feature — users, billing, settings — each exporting route config. Shared DashboardLayout wraps authenticated routes. I lazy-load heavy pages. Catch-all 404 and protected route wrapper for auth later. Central router file imports feature routes.

questions.question: User reports 404 on refresh at /dashboard/settings in production. Local dev works. What happened?
keyPoints: Not a React Router code bug alone
questions.sampleAnswer: Direct hit or refresh requests /dashboard/settings from the server. Without fallback, server looks for that file and 404s. Fix: configure server to serve index.html for all routes so React Router takes over. Vite dev hides this — production needs rewrite config.
